---
title: "Choosing a Driver"
slug: v/latest/cache/choosing-a-driver
---

`@warlock.js/cache` ships eight drivers. They all speak the same API â€” you can
swap one for another without touching call sites â€” but they trade off
durability, scale, and capabilities. This page helps you pick.

## Quick pick

| Your situation | Driver |
|---|---|
| Local dev, single process | [`memory`](/v/latest/cache/memory/) |
| Session-style data that should stay alive while used | [`memoryExtended`](/v/latest/cache/memory-extended/) |
| Bounded memory footprint (cap the entry count) | [`lru`](/v/latest/cache/lru-memory/) |
| Small persistent cache, no server (CLI, build cache) | [`file`](/v/latest/cache/file/) |
| Production, multiple instances, shared cache + locks | [`redis`](/v/latest/cache/redis/) |
| You already run Postgres, or need vector similarity | [`pg`](/v/latest/cache/pg/) |
| Disable caching without changing code | [`null`](/v/latest/cache/null/) |
| Asserting cache behavior in tests | `mock` |

## The 30-second decision

1. **Running more than one instance?** You need a *shared* store â€” go
   [`redis`](/v/latest/cache/redis/) (or [`pg`](/v/latest/cache/pg/) if
   Postgres is already in your stack). In-memory drivers are per-process, so
   each instance would hold its own copy and invalidation wouldn't propagate.
2. **Need vector similarity in production?** [`pg`](/v/latest/cache/pg/) with
   the `vector` config block â€” real pgvector index, same `cache.similar()` code.
3. **Single process, just want speed?** [`memory`](/v/latest/cache/memory/).
   Add `maxSize` to cap it, or reach for [`lru`](/v/latest/cache/lru-memory/)
   when you want a hard entry ceiling with eviction.
4. **Need it to survive a restart but don't want a server?**
   [`file`](/v/latest/cache/file/).

## Capability matrix

| Driver | Survives restart | Multi-instance | Native locks | Native lists | Similarity | Eviction |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| `memory` | âťŚ | âťŚ | emulated | read-modify-write | brute force | `maxSize` (LRU) |
| `memoryExtended` | âťŚ | âťŚ | emulated | read-modify-write | brute force | `maxSize` (LRU) |
| `lru` | âťŚ | âťŚ | emulated | read-modify-write | brute force | `capacity` (LRU) |
| `file` | âś… | âš ď¸Ź same host | emulated | read-modify-write | âťŚ | TTL only |
| `redis` | âś… | âś… | native `NX`/`XX` | read-modify-writeÂą | âťŚ *(backlog)* | TTL / Redis policy |
| `pg` | âś… | âś… | emulated | read-modify-write | âś… pgvector | TTL only |
| `null` | n/a | n/a | n/a | n/a | returns `[]` | n/a |

Âą Lists are stored as a single JSON entry and mutated read-modify-write on every
driver today. Native Redis list commands (`LPUSH` / `LRANGE` / `LTRIM`) are
planned for v2.1.

:::note `update()` / `merge()` support
The [`file`](/v/latest/cache/file/) driver throws `CacheUnsupportedError` for
`update()` and `merge()` (no atomic read-modify-write primitive on disk yet).
All other drivers support them. See
[Update & Merge](/v/latest/cache/update-merge/).
:::

## Driver one-liners

- **[`memory`](/v/latest/cache/memory/)** â€” an in-process `Map`. Fastest
  option, zero setup, gone on restart. The default for dev and single-instance
  apps. Optional `maxSize` evicts the least-recently-used entry past the limit.
- **[`memoryExtended`](/v/latest/cache/memory-extended/)** â€” `memory` with
  **sliding expiration**: every read refreshes the entry's TTL. Use it for
  data that should stay cached as long as it's actively used (session-ish
  reads) and expire only after a quiet period.
- **[`lru`](/v/latest/cache/lru-memory/)** â€” a hard `capacity` ceiling
  (default 1000) with least-recently-used eviction. Reach for it when bounding
  memory matters more than keeping every entry.
- **[`file`](/v/latest/cache/file/)** â€” JSON-on-disk. Survives restarts
  without running a server, but it's single-host and doesn't support
  `update()`/`merge()`. Good for CLIs and build caches.
- **[`redis`](/v/latest/cache/redis/)** â€” the production workhorse. Shared
  across instances, native `SET NX`/`XX` for real distributed locks, native
  list commands for O(1) list ops. Similarity isn't wired yet.
- **[`pg`](/v/latest/cache/pg/)** â€” Postgres-backed and durable. The only
  driver with a real similarity index (pgvector, HNSW or IVFFlat). Bring your
  own `pg.Pool`/`Client`; the driver doesn't own the connection lifecycle.
- **[`null`](/v/latest/cache/null/)** â€” a black hole. Every write is a no-op,
  every read a miss. Swap it in to disable caching globally without touching
  call sites.
- **`mock`** â€” an in-memory store plus a `callLog` of every operation, for
  behavioral assertions in tests. See
  [Testing](/v/latest/cache/testing/).

## Mixing drivers

You're not locked into one. Register several and route per call with the
`driver` option â€” keep your default hot path in `memory` while sending durable
or audit writes to `redis`:

```ts
await cache.set("user.1", user, "1h");                       // default driver
await cache.set("audit.event", event, { driver: "redis", ttl: "30d" });
```

See [Configuration](/v/latest/cache/configurations/) for registering drivers
and [Set Options](/v/latest/cache/set-options/#per-call-driver-override) for
per-call overrides.
